pages.text: 3. Determination of fracture energy and characteristic length Uniaxial tensile test is an ideal test to measure the Gf. On the other hand, ﬂexural tensile test, based on the measure of the total work of fracture of three-point bend notched beams, is generally used in researches due to the difﬁculty in performing stable and representative tensile tests. The fracture energy, Gf, were calcu- lated by following equation (Eq. (1)) given RILEM TC-50 FMC: Gf ¼ W0 þ mgd0 BðD /C0 aÞ ð1Þ where W0 is the energy represented by the area under the load– deﬂection curve, m is the mass of the specimen, g is the acceleration due to gravity, d0 maximum deﬂection at ﬁnal fracture (in this study, it is 10 mm), B is the beam thickness, D and a are the depth of beam and notch, respectively. mgd0 in equation of Gf represent the energy supplied from the weight of the beam itself. The characteristic length is a popular parameter, proposed by Hillerborg (Eq. (2)) [10], to measure the toughness of concrete and deﬁned as the ratio; lch ¼ EGf f 2 t ð2Þ where E is the modulus of elasticity and ft is the tensile strength. In this study, splitting tensile strength, fst was used instead of ft. Char- acteristic length is proportional to Gf and a high fracture energy leads to a tougher concrete. However, characteristic length is a marked inverse dependence on ft, and it means that a high-strength concrete is brittle. This relations show that a balance between frac- ture energy and tensile strength to obtain ductile concretes. 4. Tests results and discussion 4.1. Characteristics of fresh concrete Vebe time and unit weight test results are given in Table 3 . Using of steel ﬁbre has negative inﬂuence on the workability of concrete. Steel ﬁbres show a slowing effect to the movement of fresh concrete. Vebe time increases with the increasing of steel ﬁ- bre volume fraction for each water/cement ratio. On the other hand, the unit weight of the fresh concrete increases with the increasing of steel ﬁbre content. 4.2. Mechanical strengths and elastic modulus In general, it is known that the improvement, obtained by addi- tion of steel ﬁbre, on compressive strength of concrete is not signif- icant in comparison with its direct tensile, ﬂexural and splitting strengths. On the other hand, the addition of steel ﬁbres into con- crete may have an effect of increasing the ductility in the compres- sive failure rather than the compressive strength itself. In this research, the effects of the ﬁbre volume fraction and the ﬁbre strength on the compressive strength and modulus of elasticity are not consistent; no signiﬁcant change occurs when the ﬁbre vol- ume fraction is increased or tensile strength of ﬁbre is changed. However, the considerable increases in splitting and ﬂexural ten- sile strengths are observed by increasing ﬁbre volume fraction and ﬁbre tensile strength. Maximum increases in splitting tensile strengths are obtained at 1% ﬁbre volume fractions. For the steel ﬁ- bre with a tensile strength of 1050 MPa and 1% ﬁbre volume frac- tion, increases in splitting strengths, in comparison with plain concrete (no ﬁbre addition), are 15%, 31% and 35% for water/ce- ment ratios of 0.55, 0.45 and 0.35, respectively. Similarly, 36%, 45% and 75% increases in splitting strengths of concretes, in which steel ﬁbre with a tensile strength of 2000 MPa is used, are obtained at 1% ﬁbre volume fraction for water/cement ratios of 0.55, 0.45 and 0.35, respectively. It is need to emphasize that steel ﬁ

The inﬂuences of matrix and steel ﬁbre tensile strengths on the fracture energy of high-strength concrete Yus/C223aS/C223ahin a, Fuat Köksal b,⇑ a Department of Civil Engineering, Faculty of Civil Engineering, Istanbul Technical University, 34469 Maslak, Istanbul, Turkey b Department of Civil Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Architecture, Bozok University, 66000 Yozgat, Turkey article info Article history: Received 12 August 2010 Received in revised form 4 October 2010 Accepted 13 November 2010 Available online 10 December 2010 Keywords: High-strength concrete Steel ﬁbre Fracture energy Characteristic length Bending strength abstract This research discusses the effects of both steel ﬁbre and matrix strengths on fracture energy of high- strength concrete. The variables of experimental study were water/cement ratio, steel ﬁbre strength and steel ﬁbre volume fraction. The water/cement ratios of 0.35, 0.45 and 0.55, and steel ﬁbres with a tensile strengths of 1100 and 2000 MPa were used and volume fractions of steel ﬁbre were 0.33%, 0.67% and 1%. Mechanical properties, fracture energy and characteristic length of concretes were inves- tigated. Signiﬁcant inﬂuences of matrix and ﬁbre tensile strengths on the fracture energy and the characteristic length are noted. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction It is well-known that the addition of steel ﬁbres into concrete at a certain volume fraction improves the ductility of concrete [1–4] . The major effect of steel ﬁbres randomly distributed in matrix can be seen after matrix cracking. Steel ﬁbres bridge gap between adja- cent surfaces of existing micro-crack, delay crack formation and limit crack propagation by reducing the crack tip opening displace- ment [5,6]. This mechanism is known as bridging mechanism .I n addition to effect of crack bridging, steel ﬁbre–matrix bond strength also contribute to the increasing fracture toughness of the steel ﬁbre reinforced concrete (SFRC). Other factors such as ﬁ- bre type and orientation of ﬁbres in matrix, aspect ratio (length/ diameter), volume fraction and tensile strength of ﬁbre as well as matrix strength inﬂuence the performance of SFRC [7–9] . Fracture energy of concrete is one of the most important param- eter in understanding the properties of concrete and determining the design criteria of large concrete structures. The fracture energy (G f) is deﬁned as the area under the load–deﬂection curve per unit fractured surface area. The most widely used fracture mechanics models for analyzing concrete structures is the ﬁctitious crack model (FCM) proposed by Hillerborg et al. [10–12] . RILEM [13,14] and Petersson [15] recommended a method for the deter- mination of Gf using simple three-point bending test. The fracture energies of high-strength steel ﬁbre reinforced concretes (HSFRCs), tested in this experimental study, were evaluated using the equa- tion given by RILEM, in which toughness was calculated from the load–deﬂection curves obtained by performing a third-point test on notched beam in accordance with EN14651 [16]. The main objective in this research is to determine the effects of both matrix and ﬁbre tensile strengths on fracture energy and mechanical properties of HSFRCs and emphasize the importance and dependency of tensile strength of steel ﬁbre to the matrix strength. The opening and propagation of the crack are controlled by the steel ﬁbres along the fracture plane. In many studies, it is pointed out that ﬁb

strengths is required and it must be taken into consideration in de- sign of steel ﬁbre reinforced concrete mixes to maximize the frac- ture energy and minimize the cost of mix. Fracture energies and characteristic lengths of concretes, calcu- lated by using Eqs. (1) and (2) , are given in Table 3. Fracture ener- gies of concretes with a high ﬁbre strength are higher than that of the concrete with a low ﬁbre strength for each water/cement ratio. Fig. 5a and b shows the fracture energies of concrete depending on the ﬁbre volume fraction and water/cement ratio for ﬁbre strengths of 1050 MPa and 2000 MPa, respectively. As it is shown in ﬁgures, steel ﬁbres with a low tensile strength exhibit a good performance, resulting in high fracture energy values, for low ma- trix strengths. However, steel ﬁbres with a high tensile strength are more effective for high matrix strength in point of fracture energy. On the other hand, characteristic length l ch, which is a popular parameter for a measure of brittleness of concrete, increases by decreasing water/cement ratio or increasing matrix strength for the ﬁbre tensile strength of 1050 MPa. Maximum characteristic length values of SFRCs with a 2000 MPa ﬁbre tensile strength are obtained at the water/cement ratio of 0.45. Fig. 6 a and b shows the changes in characteristic lengths of concretes depending on the ﬁbre volume fraction and water/cement ratio for ﬁbre strengths of 1050 MPa and 2000 MPa, respectively. Depending on the results of fracture energy and characteristic length, it can be emphasized that matrix and steel ﬁbre tensile strengths are two parameters playing an important role in performance of SFRCs and must be taken into account in mix design. 5. Conclusions and remarks From the test results, steel ﬁbre volume fraction and steel ﬁbre strength have no signiﬁcant effect on compressive strength and modulus of elasticity of concrete. However, for splitting and ﬂex- ural strengths, considerable improvements are obtained and steel ﬁbres with a high tensile strength are more effective than that of the ﬁbre of the low ones. It has also been observed that the higher 4000 8000 12000 16000 20000 24000 Water/Cement Ratio Water/Cement Ratio f su = 1050 MPa 00 4000 8000 12000 16000 20000 24000 0.35 0.45 0.55 0.35 0.45 0.55 Fracture Energy (GF), N/m ), N/mFractur Energy (GF f su = 2000 MPVf = 1.00% Vf = 0.67% Vf = 0.33% Vf = 1.00% Vf = 0.67% Vf = 0.33% Fig. 5. Characteristic lengths of SFRCs depending on water/cement ratio and ﬁbre volume fraction. Y. S/C223ahin, F. Köksal / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1801–1806 1805

(4–12 mm and 12–19 mm) were used as ﬁne size and coarse size aggregate, respec- tively. Speciﬁc gravity of ﬁne and coarse aggregates were 2.71 and 2.74, respec- tively. High-range water reducing admixture was used to observe the effects of steel ﬁbre in workability of fresh concrete. Cold drawn steel ﬁbres with low and high carbon, with hooked-ends, were used and their properties are given in Table 1. The water/cement ( W/C) ratios of 0.35, 0.45 and 0.55 were used and volume fractions (V f) of steel ﬁbre in concrete were 0.33%, 0.67% and 1%. Vebe time of con- trol concretes (no ﬁbre addition) for each water/cement ratio was kept constant as 4 s by changing the amount of water reducer. A total of 21 concrete mixtures including control groups were produced in this experimental research. The mix pro- portions of concrete series are given in Table 2 . 2.2. Test specimens: preparation and testing For a uniform mixture, cement, silica fume and all aggregates were blended ﬁrst and then, the mixture of water and high-range water reducing admixture was added to the mixture. Finally, steel ﬁbres were carefully scattered to the mixture to provide a uniform distribution of ﬁbres in the mixture. The specimens were cast in steel moulds and compacted on a vibration table. All the specimens were demoulded after about 24 h and exposed to 28 days standard water curing. Vebe time test to observe the chances in workability due to the use of steel ﬁbre and unit weight test were made on fresh concretes. Three cylinders, 150 mm in diameter and 300 mm in height, were used for com- pressive strength and modulus of elasticity tests. The splitting tensile strength test was performed on three disc specimens with a diameter of 100 mm and a thickness of 60 mm. The ﬂexural tensile strength test was performed on 150 /C2 150 /C2 700 mm 3 prismatic notched-specimens by using the beam method according to EN14651 standard at which load is applied at one third points of the specimen [16]. Two prismatic notched-specimens, with an effective span of 500 mm, were tested by using a closed loop deﬂection-controlled loading frame of 250 kN capacity and loading rate was 0.2 mm/min. Central notches, with a depth of 25 mm and width of 3 mm were made using a diamond saw. Test setup is given in Fig. 1 . The load and the mid-span deﬂection of the beams were simultaneously recorded and also, load–deﬂection curves for each specimen was also obtained graphically during the test. Table 1 Properties of hooked-end steel ﬁbres used. 2000 MPa, are obtained at 1% ﬁbre volume fraction for water/ce- ment ratios of 0.55, 0.45 and 0.35, respectively. The ﬂexural strengths of concretes, in which high tensile strength steel ﬁbres are used, are higher than that of the concrete with a low tensile strength steel ﬁbres. High-strength steel ﬁbres are more effective in improvement of ﬂexural strength of concrete for each water/ce- ment ratio, although the performance class of both high and low tensile strength steel ﬁbres are the same. 4.3. Fracture energy and characteristic length Steel ﬁbres along the fracture plane control the opening and propagation of the crack. Examinations made on fracture planes after completion of tests, it is seen that some ﬁbres are broken but some of them are pulled-out of the matrix during the crack propagation after matrix cracking. In concretes with a low water/ cement ratio or a high matrix strength, steel ﬁbres with a low ten- sile strength exhibit a broken behaviour into two parts, however, it is observed that steel ﬁbres with a high tensile strength are pulled- out from matrix. Steel ﬁbres, with high or low tensile strength, generally show a behaviour of pulled-out of matrix for high water/cement ratio or a low matrix strength. It is concluded that the performance of steel ﬁbres in slowing the propagation and bridging of the cracks, or softening branch of the load–deﬂection curve, is related to the matrix strength as well as ﬁbre volume frac- tion. Load–deﬂection curves for water/cement ratio of 0.55. 1804 Y. S/C223ahin, F. Köksal / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1801–1806

ﬁbre volume fraction produces lesser step descending branch of load–deﬂection curves resulting in higher ductility and fracture en- ergy of high-strength concrete. On the other hand, very signiﬁcant inﬂuences of matrix and ﬁbre tensile strengths on the fracture en- ergy and characteristic length are noted. The importance of rela- tion between matrix and ﬁbre tensile strengths appears after peak load and the softening part of load–deﬂection curve is a yield of that relation. The bond strength between matrix and steel ﬁbre decides the behaviour of ﬁbre crossing the crack or both matrix and ﬁbre strengths control this behaviour. There are two case for ﬁbres during crack propagation: steel ﬁbres may be broken into two parts or pulled-out from matrix. This situation directly affect the fracture toughness or energy absorption capacity of SFRCs. A dominant ﬁber–matrix interface is required to prevent the com- plete ﬁbre pull-out from matrix without rupture and to increase the crack bridging performance of SFRCs. It is also emphasized that the bond between steel ﬁbre and matrix takes an important role in improving ductility, ﬁrst crack strength and ﬂexural strength of SFRCs. The effectiveness of tensile strength of steel ﬁbre in crack bridging performance of high-strength steel ﬁbre reinforced con- cretes is clearly showed by this study and the use of high tensile strength ﬁbre is very essential to increase the capacity of fracture energy of high-strength concrete. The experimental results indi- cate that the compatibility between the matrix and the steel ﬁbre is so important for postcracking behaviour and fracture energy enhancement of high-strength concrete and ﬁbre strength is a con- trolling factor in the performance of SFRCs, and so a comprehensive understanding of the steel ﬁbre characteristics is required. As a re- sult, it is clearly seen that matrix and ﬁbre strengths are effective parameters for the mechanical properties, especially fracture en- ergy of SFRCs. In the mix design of more ductile concretes incorpo- rating steel ﬁbre, both of water/cement ratio and ﬁbre strength must be taken as the mix design parameters for the performance and economical points of view.
